title: Michigan sees explosive outbreak of diarrheal parasite with over 700 cases
gist: Michigan has recorded over 700 Cyclospora cayetanensis cases and 36 hospitalizations since June 22 with no identified produce source, a tally wildly out of line with the state's typical ~50/year, while the CDC's national surveillance dashboard remains frozen at June 16 data and excludes Michigan entirely amid unanswered questions to HHS.

insights.claim: Michigan reported over 700 cyclosporiasis cases and 36 hospitalizations since June 22, with cases climbing from 170 on June 30 to 572 on July 4—an explosive two-week rise.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: A rapidly scaling foodborne outbreak with an unknown source poses recall and liability risk to whichever produce supplier is eventually implicated.
insights.quote: As of July 6, the state has received reports of over 700 cases since June 22, along with 36 hospitalizations.
insights.locator: Cases of a diarrhea-causing intestinal parasite

insights.claim: Michigan's tally is far out of line with its own history, which has mostly been around 50 cases a year with a prior high of 97 in 2023.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: The order-of-magnitude anomaly signals a genuine point-source contamination event rather than background variation.
insights.quote: In the past several years, the state has mostly reported totals of around 50 cases a year, with a high of 97 in 2023.

insights.claim: No specific produce grower, supplier, or product has been identified as the source, though previous outbreaks were linked to bagged salad mixes, cilantro, basil, raspberries, snow peas, and green onions.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Fresh-produce categories with prior Cyclospora links are the leading recall candidates to monitor as the investigation narrows.
insights.quote: At this time, no specific produce grower, supplier or type of produce has been identified as the source.

insights.claim: The CDC's cyclosporiasis surveillance website only has case reporting up to June 16 and includes zero cases from Michigan, and HHS did not respond to questions about why the data hasn't been updated.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: A stale federal surveillance system during an active outbreak is a systemic public-health data-integrity concern with implications for outbreak-response readiness.
insights.quote: But it only has case reporting up to June 16 and doesn't include any cases from Michigan.
insights.locator: The CDC has a website for cyclosporiasis surveillance

insights.claim: Using the CDC's outdated numbers, 17 states besides Michigan reported 145 cases nationally this year; adding Michigan pushes the total over 845.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Michigan alone dwarfs the rest of the reported national count, underscoring how concentrated and anomalous this outbreak is.
insights.locator: According to the CDC's outdated numbers

insights.claim: Nationally the US has recorded between 2,000 and 5,000 cyclosporiasis cases annually since 2018 with cases historically peaking in June and July, so the US total so far is not out of line despite Michigan's record.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Context that the national trend is seasonal-normal isolates Michigan as the outlier and focuses source-tracing on that state's supply chain.
insights.quote: Since 2018, the US has been recording between 2,000 and 5,000 cases each year.
insights.locator: While it may be a record year
